article_title: CDT Study Catalogs 37 'Dark Patterns' Across AI Chatbots, From ChatGPT and Claude to Replika and Character.AI

content_preview.title: CDT Study Catalogs 37 'Dark Patterns' Across AI Chatbots, From ChatGPT and Claude to Replika and Character.AI
content_preview.summary: A Center for Democracy & Technology study built a taxonomy of 37 manipulative design patterns it found across major AI chatbots and companion apps.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Researchers at the Center for Democracy & Technology examined widely used AI chatbots and concluded that manipulative interface design is now pervasive across the category. In a study titled "Dark Patterns in AI Chatbots: A Taxonomy to Inform Better Design," they looked at general-purpose assistants including ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude, along with companion bots such as Replika and Character.AI, and built a taxonomy of 37 dark patterns applicable to AI chatbots, [404 Media reported...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (608 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / The Examples / Background / What We Don't Know sections. Appropriately hedged: it never overstates what the two accessible outlets establish, and the closing section transparently flags what 404 Media's coverage does not enumerate.
editor_notes.source_verification: Both source snapshots read from disk and verified. source-0.html.gz (404 Media, 'New Study Reveals the Manipulative Dark Patterns of AI Chatbots', Samantha Cole, May 29, 2026, HTTP 200) confirms: study title 'Dark Patterns in AI Chatbots: A Taxonomy to Inform Better Design'; the figure of a 'taxonomy of 37 dark patterns'; all three author names (Ruchika Joshi, Adinawa Adjagbodjou, Michal Luria); the products studied (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Replika, Character.AI); Luria's title (senior research fellow); all three Luria/researcher quotes verbatim ('It was surprising to see...financial loss', 'When we think about AI chatbots...the patterns themselves', 'Dark patterns do not operate only where users are unaware...'); and both concrete examples (Cute AI 'still leave cruelly'; Meta AI 'spill the tea' / 'Cross my heart, won't tell a soul'). source-1.html.gz (TechCrunch, 'AI sycophancy isn't just a quirk...', Aug 25, 2025, HTTP 200) confirms the Webb Keane attributions: anthropology professor, author of 'Animals, Robots, Gods', characterizing sycophancy as a 'dark pattern' defined as 'a deceptive design choice that manipulates users for profit', and the verbatim quote 'It's a strategy to produce this addictive behavior, like infinite scrolling.' No archive fallback; both fetched live at 200.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific (the 37 figure, three author names, five product names, Luria's title, two interface examples, the Keane book title and quotes, the TechCrunch definition of 'dark pattern') traces to its attributed outlet. 404 Media is dated May 29, 2026 and states the study 'was published Friday'; May 29, 2026 is a Friday, so the article's 'published in late May 2026' is accurate and suitably conservative. No hallucinations detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Manipulative-design claims are consistently attributed to the cited outlets or the researchers rather than asserted in the Herald's own voice. No sensationalism or editorializing; no AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior Herald coverage of the CDT dark-patterns taxonomy ('taxonomy of 37' and 'Center for Democracy' dark-patterns searches return no matching article). The internal cross-reference to the May 26 GUARD Act piece (companion-chatbot regulation) is a distinct, complementary story; this article does not duplicate it. The GUARD Act link target exists at src/content/articles/2026-05/26-senate-judiciary-committee-unanimously-advances-guard-act-to-ban-ai-companion-chatbots-for-minors.md and resolves.
concerns: Two sources is on the lower end. Both, however, are allowlisted (404media.co, techcrunch.com) and the central thesis is carried entirely by the accessible, allowlisted 404 Media; TechCrunch supplies only the historical 'dark pattern'/sycophancy framing in the Background section. The primary CDT report (cdt.org) is off-allowlist and returns HTTP 403, so it was correctly not cited; the headline, summary, and lead rest only on 404 Media, not on the blocked CDT source.

The five taxonomy category names appear only in the blocked CDT source and were correctly OMITTED from the article body; the piece never enumerates them and explicitly notes in 'What We Don't Know' that 404's coverage does not list all 37 patterns or give a per-product breakdown.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. The central thesis — that a CDT study built a 37-item taxonomy of dark patterns observed across major chatbots and companion apps — is fully supported by the allowlisted 404 Media snapshot, and the Background framing is fully supported by the allowlisted TechCrunch snapshot. No claim depends on the blocked CDT source. Every quote is verbatim, every specific is sourced, the headline/summary/lead are backed by 404 Media, both body URLs are present in article.sources, and the GUARD Act cross-reference resolves. Clean APPROVE under the v3.9.0 workflow.
